blocks.content: The streets were always quieter before the world decided to wake up.
Ethan Carter liked them that way.

No car horns. No crowded sidewalks. No strangers brushing past him with somewhere better to be. Just the pale blue edge of morning stretching itself over the rooftops, the soft slap of his running shoes against pavement, and the steady rhythm of his breathing.
It was the one part of the day that never asked anything from him.

He turned the corner past Bellamy Street, slowing only when the road sloped downward toward the older part of town. The buildings here leaned close together, brick walls marked by weather, faded signs, and ivy that had long ago decided it belonged wherever it wanted.
Ethan noticed things like that.

The cracked green paint on the bookstore door. The way steam curled from the bakery vents before anyone unlocked the front. The orange cat that slept in the window of the antique shop like it owned the entire block.
Most people walked past those things.
Ethan collected them.
Not with his hands, but with his eyes. With his sketchbook. With paint-stained fingers and quiet hours spent trying to put feeling into color.

He slowed near the corner café, one hand resting against the stitch in his side. The sign above the door read The Copper Mug in chipped gold lettering. Lights were already glowing inside, though the place would not officially open for another thirty minutes.
Same place. Same morning. Same order waiting in his mind.
Black coffee, two sugars, and the corner table near the window.
Predictable.
Safe.
That’s probably why I like it,
he thought.

Ethan stretched his arms above his head and exhaled, watching his breath disappear into the cool morning air. He was nineteen, but most days he felt both older and younger than that. Old enough to know dreams cost money. Young enough to keep pretending that mattered less than it did.
Freelance artist sounded nice when people asked what he did.
It sounded intentional. Independent. Creative.
It sounded better than,
I paint whatever people will pay for and hope it’s enough to keep going.

He had done pet portraits, café menu boards, birthday signs, a mural for a children’s room, and one very strange commission from a man who wanted his motorcycle painted as if it were “riding through outer space.”
Ethan still wasn’t sure if it had turned out good or if the man had simply been too kind to say otherwise.
He smiled faintly at the memory, then looked down the street as the first bus hissed to a stop near the curb.
A few passengers stepped off.
A man in a gray suit. A woman with grocery bags. A teenager with headphones.
Then her.
Red hair first.
That was what Ethan noticed before anything else.

Not bright in a loud way. Not fake. Just vivid, like autumn had decided to show up early and take human form. She stepped down from the bus with a paper cup in one hand and a backpack hanging from one shoulder, moving like the morning belonged to her.

She wasn’t dressed fancy. White sneakers, jeans, a soft green jacket, and a bracelet that flashed silver when she lifted her drink. But there was something about her that made the quiet street feel suddenly less quiet.
She laughed at something on her phone.
Not a polite laugh.
A real one.
The kind that slipped out before a person could stop it.
Ethan looked away too quickly.
Don’t be weird.
He bent down, pretending to retie a shoe that wasn’t untied.

When he glanced back, she was crossing the street toward the arcade two blocks down, the old one with the flickering neon sign that still promised TOKENS — PRIZES — FUN like the world had never moved on.
Ethan watched for half a second longer than he should have.
She’s beautiful.
The thought came without permission.
He swallowed and shook his head.
And you have absolutely no chance with someone like that.
That thought came easier.
More familiar.
He started running again before he could embarrass himself by standing there any longer.
